profile.name: Faiz Mustansar
profile.headline: What I learned from my co-op
profile.subhead: A short, visual recap of growth, craft, and how I think now.

sections.headline: Before
bullets: I wasn’t doing much outside of school. Just mindlessly studying for exams and barely getting by.
In Jan 2024 I attended a GDSC event and met someone without a co-op program who still landed TD, Interac, Amazon, and now works at Google. It motivated me.
That’s when I started going all in: projects, hackathons, and actually building instead of only studying.
I even cross-enrolled at the University of Waterloo and pushed myself into harder rooms.
Later I got accepted to Hack the North, and that chain reaction is how I eventually got into TD.

photos.captionBody: one of the most impactful events of my life as I learned that even without a co-op, you can still land a job at a big company.
I was just studying for the sake of studying, not for the sake of learning.
I built my first website in 2024 and it turned out to be ok
I attended my first hackathon in 2024 and it was a great learning experience.
I got my first job at my university as a undergraduate researcher in 2024 and it was a great learning experience.
I started to build projects and contribute to open source projects in 2024
I built my first project with real users in 2025. gained over 1k users,25+ views and managed a team of 5 people
I enrolled into three different universities in 2025. uw was for networking and learning, guelph was for easy courses and ottawa is where i had to complete a mandatory course
playing in 7 leagues during summer 2025 even tho i was doing 7 courses across 3 universities
accepted into canada's biggest hackathon. this is how i landed my first real job at td bank!
more projects, more users, more views

sections.headline: During
bullets: Reviews trained my taste: clarity, edge cases, and long-term maintainability.
Ownership meant closing loops: spec, ship, monitor, and iterate.
I learned to communicate decisions and tradeoffs clearly.

photos.captionBody: I started treating review comments as a map of what great looks like and how to get there.
Shipping was the easy part. The real work was understanding failure modes and follow-through.

sections.headline: After
bullets: I now default to data, tests, and small iterations over assumptions.
I think in systems: performance, reliability, observability, and humans.
I write with the reader in mind: PRs, docs, and decisions.

photos.captionBody: From making it work to making it robust, understandable, and easy to change.

sections.headline: Next steps
bullets: Keep building projects with real constraints and real users.
Go deeper on systems thinking and performance engineering.
Stay consistent: ship, learn, write, repeat.

photos.captionBody: I want the next thing I build to be smaller, sharper, and more measurable.
